chunk_summary: Discharge decisions in outpatient CMAM programs in India should prioritize admission criteria and edema-free stability, ensuring both MUAC and WHZ recovery align to prevent residual risks.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of using admission criteria for discharge decisions in CMAM programs.
Learn the WHO recovery standards for MUAC and WHZ/WLZ in severe acute malnutrition management.
Identify strategies to address discordant recovery between MUAC and WHZ measurements.
Explore the role of functional recovery indicators in discharge decisions.
key_facts: MUAC <115 mm, WHZ/WLZ <-3, or nutritional edema are admission criteria for CMAM.
WHO recovery standards: MUAC ≥125 mm and no bilateral pitting edema for two consecutive visits, or WHZ/WLZ ≥-2 and no edema for two consecutive visits.
Discordant recovery between MUAC and WHZ is common and requires continued therapeutic feeding and reassessment.
Residual muscle wasting indicated by WHZ < -2 carries substantial mortality risk even if MUAC normalizes.
In India, documenting both MUAC and weight-for-height during clinic visits reduces discharge errors across systems.
reference_quote: A safe WHO recovery standard is: MUAC ≥125 mm and no bilateral pitting edema for at least two consecutive visits, or WHZ/WLZ ≥-2 and no edema for at least two consecutive visits, depending on the program pathway. (Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK601654/)
document_key_topics: Discharge criteria for MUAC and WHZ discordance
